title: Root
aliases: Root: A Game of Woodland Might and Right

component_identification.content: Root uses a shared woodland map, shared deck, ambush and dominance cards, faction boards, faction warriors and tokens, crafted item tokens, and battle dice. Base game includes four asymmetric factions with unique pieces and setup. Cards have fox, rabbit, mouse, or bird suits, with bird often wild under core rules. Faction boards define each faction action structure and scoring pattern and must be complete for legal play.

core_game_loop.content: Turns proceed clockwise. Each turn has Birdsong, Daylight, and Evening. Players resolve faction effects in Birdsong, perform main actions and crafting in Daylight, then draw and clean up in Evening. Shared core systems are movement, battle, crafting, and ruling clearings, but each faction modifies them heavily. Game ends when a player reaches 30 points or fulfills a dominance condition.

detailed_rules.content: The Law of Root defines precedence: cards override general rules and cannot rules are absolute unless explicitly superseded. Control of clearings drives movement and action legality. Battle uses two dice with hits capped by warriors present, and piece removal can score based on faction and target type. Ambush cards can modify battle timing. Cards come from shared draw and discard piles; hand size is public but card identity is private unless revealed by effect. Bird suit is wild for many costs but can be forced as prompted suit by specific effects. Crafting requires faction-specific crafting piece presence in matching suits. Dominance cards replace normal point victory with map-control objectives. Key faction management issues are decree stability for Eyrie, action economy buildings for Cats, supporter and outrage flow for Alliance, and item and relationship economy for Vagabond. Common disputes involve simultaneous effects, movement legality, and faction text overrides.

scoring_and_endgame.content: Standard victory is immediate at 30 points. Alternative victory is dominance: a player who has switched to dominance wins by meeting that card objective at the required timing. Points are earned through faction actions, cardboard removal, crafting, and faction-specific effects. Always check win state immediately when points are gained.

beginner_strategy.content: Play your faction plan, not generic value moves. Remove enemy scoring infrastructure, not just warriors. Track leader pace and table balance early because interaction decides outcomes. Craft when it supports your faction tempo rather than as a side hobby. Plan one turn cycle ahead so suit constraints do not trap your action economy.

metadata.notes: Rewrite sprint with rulebook-text as primary source.
